sentences.text: My name is Laura Oberlender, L-A-U-R-A O-B-E-R-L-E-N-D-E-R.
August 5, '34.
I was born in Tuczyn, Poland.
We were very close-- a very close family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
In 1939, the Russians took over our part of Poland when the war started, and we had the Russians with us till '41.
And at that time, we had moved into our new home.
And my father was very much afraid that they would-- that we would be send away to Siberia, because my father employed people working for him.
And so we were considered wealthy.
And the Russians, they didn't like that.
So we were very much afraid that we would be send away.
So every night, my parents would say goodbye to all their relatives.
And during the night, they would come and drag you in-- in trucks and take you away.
So that was a fear.
That you would be taken away?
Right.
It never happened, but maybe had it happened, we would have been better off.
But it did not happen.
But we were very fearful of that, until the Germans marched in in '41.
When the Germans marched in, the first week, they had a pogrom.
And because my-- my-- our home was such a-- it was a new building.
It was such a beautiful home, that it attracted attention.
Even though our name was not on the list for the pogrom, we were-- we were, you know, the German soldier-- we were looking out through the window.
And we saw that he said, well, what about here?
And so they said, the Ukrainians, OK.
Let's go.
There was a whole bunch of them with a few soldiers-- German soldiers.
So there was a big pogrom a week after they entered our town.
And my mother and I were beaten up terribly.
They threw my mother back into the courtyard, and they beat her with-- with guns.
She had-- I remember my sister and I were standing on the doorway, and we were screaming.
And her blood was gushing out of her head, and I remember that sound.
It went like sszzhh-- that kind of a sound.
And after a while, she stopped screaming, and they said, well, you know, she's done.
So my sister pulled me away from the doorway.
And the next thing I know, I was lying under a bed in a bedroom.
And apparently, they were looking for me.
And I must have been unconscious, I don't know.
But I opened up my eyes, and I tried to get up, and I tried to say, here I am.
And I realized that nothing was coming out of my mouth, and I couldn't move.
There was something lying under the bed, and I tried to reach it so I could get up.
And I couldn't do that.
And I probably went-- was unconscious again, off and on, and-- because I remember waking up a few times.
And they were looking for me.
My mother's brother, Friedel, came to help.
And the next thing I knew, I was lying on a couch in the living room.
And I was covered with a sheet, or the-- I think I had a-- like a sitter under my head.
And my father was crying.
And they said-- and my uncle and my father thought I was dead.
And at that time, I was seven years old.
And I thought they're going to bury me alive.
I was like so frightened.
I kept thinking, oh, my God, I'm alive here.
I'm-- I'm alive.
You know, don't leave me!
And they kept looking at me and covering me up.
And they were busy with my mother, because she had seven holes in her head, and they crushed her-- all her bones in her head.
And she had a broken bone right over her eye and her nose.
And-- and they were trying to, you know, treat her and-- and tried to wash all the blood away from her, as she was gushing with blood.
And they left me alone.
And I thought, you know, that was the end of me.
Luckily, my sister, some-- for some reason or another, wasn't even touched.
But my mother had a friend visiting-- it was on a Friday night-- and her baby was killed in the house, a nine-month old baby.
And that was the beginning of, you know.
So, of course, the next morning, my father gathered up my mother and I and my sister, and he moved us into a poor section of the town.
And we were not allowed to get any medical attention.
And then we found out later on that many people were killed and injured.
But my mother and I were one of the most severely injured.
And my-- I must have had a blow to my head so severe that it probably caused like a stroke on my right side, because I was paralyzed for almost a year.
And my head was like a ball, very soft.
And-- and my whole face started-- it was swollen.
And I closed my eyes.
They were all swollen.
And I remember lying in bed with my mother, and she was all bandaged up.
And she was praying, please God, let her-- let her die.
She's deaf, and mute, and paralyzed, and-- but my mind was working.
I knew what was going on, but I couldn't communicate for a long time.
And at that time is when we met one of my father's clients that he used to deal with.
You know, he was a farmer that lived three kilometers from-- from our town in Szubkow.
And he came.
He must have heard what had happened.
So he came, and he looked at my mother and I, and he was like crying.
He couldn't believe that was-- this happened.
And I have-- I heard him say, well, what did this child do to deserve this?
And he started coming around, bringing eggs and butter once in a while.
And this is how our friendship started with him.
And what was his name?
Do you remember?
His name was Pavlo Garasimchuk.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
It existed for over a year, all through the time the Germans were in and out of our town.
They set up-- it was called a Kehillah.
Do you ever hear that, Kehillah?
It's like a Jewish government.
They appointed a few people.
And when they drove in with the trucks, the Germans, they would demand certain things they wanted from the people in the town.
They would want, like, say, diamonds, or furs, or jewelry, gold, all kinds of stuff.
And these people knew everyone in town, and they would go around collecting.
And they said if they don't get their things within a certain amount of time, they're going to take 40, 50 people and kill.
So every-- so, you know, this was the Kehillah.
And it was a very frightening time.
And they would come in every few weeks.
And this is-- was the standard, because it was going on all the time.
During that time, my father-- we had moved back into our house.
And my father was working with me all the time.

And then, gradually, I started walking again, but my right arm, till this day, is not 100%, because I couldn't-- I started using my left hand, and I became left-handed, because I couldn't use my right hand.
So my father built hiding places through-- in-- in our house-- under the steps, in the attic, all over the place.
So when they would come in with the trucks, the Germans and the Gestapo, we would run and hide most of the time.
So that was going on for about a year, a year and a 1/2.
Until in 1942, they-- they made the ghetto.
They created the ghetto.
And we knew that was the very end.
That was right before they liquidated the whole town.
Not for very long, no.
Well, we-- at the time, they took like 40 or 50 houses in the very poor section of our town, and they build a fence.
And this was the ghetto.
And they said by a certain date, everyone had to be inside the ghetto, otherwise, you'll be killed.
And we knew that this was the end, because we heard other towns, and so we knew it was-- it was-- it was the end.
But people are very foolish.
I don't know, they still think there's always hope.
People were dragging all their belongings through the ghetto.
They were moving in.
They were bringing their-- all their valuables with them.
And my parents were packing too.
My grandmother's house was in the ghetto, in this section.
So we were moving into the grandmother's-- my grandmother's house.
And at that time, there was a principal from Czechoslovakia who wasn't Jewish.
And the Germans told him to go and look for a home.
You can pick any house you want, and of course, he picked our home.
So he-- they came.
They had no children.
And they saw us packing, and they said, you know you're going to be killed very soon.
And they fell in love with my sister.
She was blonde and blue eyes, and she did not look Jewish.
And they said, we will adopt her.
Maybe, you know, maybe it's so that she should-- she's going to survive.
So at first, my parents wouldn't hear of it.
They said, of course not.
You know, they wouldn't hear of it.
And we moved into the ghetto.
And that same day, like the next day, the ghetto was going to be closed.
And there was wall-to-wall people all over the-- all over the ghetto.
They were bringing in from small villages, all surroundings.
And you could see that it was just a matter of days.
And the Ukraine people, they were waiting with sacks outside the fence.
And as soon as the people would be liquidated, they would have, you know, stuff their bags with all the gold and everything that was in the ghetto.
There was a lot of stuff.
Everyone was bringing all their good stuff-- their good stuff with them.
So my father remembered Pavlo.
And we tried to make a run for it.
And we almost succeeded in getting out of there.
I mean, I don't know where we were running to, but just to get away from the ghetto.
And we were walking, and then we had to cross over a small little lake.
And there were some teenagers-- young teenagers-- Christians, Ukraines.
And they demanded my father a lot of money, otherwise, they're going to drown us in the little boat.
There was a little boat.
They took us on a little boat that crossed over.
And every time, they started shaking the boat-- and more money, more money.
And it was-- I remember, we were-- I was crying.
It was very, very fearful at that time.
And so, finally, we crossed over.
And they started chasing us.
And they called the police and the Germans.
And they caught us.
I don't know why they didn't kill us right there and then.
But they beat us up, and they took us back to the ghetto
My mother and father, and my sister.
The four of us.
Somehow, Pavlo must have heard about it, because it was not far from his farm.
So we came back to the ghetto.
And my mother and father decided to take my sister over to the principal's house.
They figured, well, maybe it's her fate to survive.
And I remember, it was so-- [LAUGHS] --I was about seven years old.
And I said, Hannahla, don't go.
And she said to me-- those were her last words-- I'll do whatever Mom and Dad say I should do.
She was 11.
Or maybe-- yeah, 11.
And my mother dressed her up.
And I remember she made her two big red bows, like, you know, ribbons in her hair.
And she looked so pretty.
And my father took her over.
And the next day, 12 o'clock, there was the-- they were supposed to close the gates of the ghetto, and she left her there.
And when he went-- came back, in no time, Pavlo came into the ghetto.
It was bashert-- bashert that she shouldn't survive.
It was so close.
And he brought peasant clothes with him.
He said, I can't take you-- to my father-- but I can-- I can try to smuggle your wife and children out of the ghetto as peasants, like my wife and children.
So he dressed us up in his wife's and children's clothes-- my mother and I.
My sister was gone already.
And we slowly marched out of the ghetto, and we were able to get to this farm with no problem at all.
The next morning, they closed the ghetto much earlier than they were supposed to.
My father was in the ghetto, and he knew my sister Hannah was going to come any minute, and she would be caught.
So as soon as they closed the ghetto, all of a sudden, everyone started jumping.
I mean, young boys started jumping out through the fence trying to escape.
The Gestapo was all over them outside.
And they were shooting left and right and-- and were being killed.
My father, who is-- [LAUGHS] not a big sportsman-- but he just like didn't care, because he wanted to save my sister-- he made-- he jumped through the fence.
And for some reason, you know, it's like a miracle.
The Gestapo turned his head.
Maybe he sneezed, or whatever, and my father was able to run across.
And he was able to get to our home, and just in time to warn her not to come.
And he told the people that he was going back to the-- to the ghetto.
But in fact, he went outside, and he climbed under the steps, which we had a hiding place, and he hid there.
And he told my sister that that's where he was going to be hiding.
In the meantime, all the people in from the ghetto got a hold of gasoline.
And that night, they-- the whole ghetto burned.
You don't hear about that, because it was a small little town.
Tuczyn.
Tuczyn.
And the whole ghetto, all 50 or 60 homes, were burning.
It was a big fire.
And so no one could come in to-- to get all their things.
They wanted at least-- you know, they saw what was going on.
And the Gestapo couldn't get in either.
So they were just shooting, and people were running.
About 3,000 people were able to escape into the woods.
But unfortunately, at the end of the war, they were all killed.
And my mother and I saw the big-- we saw the big fire at night.
He woke us up, and he said, something is going on in Tuczyn.
And he got scared, Pavlo.
So he took us, and he put is in the barn.
And-- and we were there for about a week.
We had no idea what was going on.
He's-- he told us later that the ghetto burned, and everyone was killed.
And a lot of Jews went into the woods, and they were chasing them.
And the-- and the Ukraine, you know, the young teenagers and the young men, whatever.
It was-- it was like a big sport to go in the woods and chase the Jews and kill them, and so forth.
It was-- it was wintertime.
It was in September.
And there was snow.
So there were sleds.
You could-- you couldn't very well hide without, you know, footprints, and all that.
So after about a week, they said that they're not going to kill anymore.
They-- they had a big sign of-- they announced that whoever survived this can come back, you know, and everything is going to be fine.
And many people did come back, because they had no place to go.
They had no food.
They had no shelter.
And-- and for-- they just came back, of course.
They rounded up again, I don't know how many hundreds of people.
At that time-- in the meantime, my father was in that hole for one week, and my sister was given--
Outside.
It was under the steps.
We had like four or five steps that go into the house.
Underneath the steps.
He took out one step-- was taken out, and he crawled in.
That was his hiding place.
He was there for one week.
And my sister kept him alive.
She gave him food for one week.
When she would be eating her meals, she'd stick a piece of bread under her sleeve, or something, you know?
And she would-- and they-- they never knew that my father was hiding underneath the steps there.
And my sister was keeping him alive.
September.
It was right-- right after Yom Kippur.
So after a week, when they announced that the Jews can come out freely-- they were not going to be killed anymore.
My father came out and made pretend like he survived.
And he came into the house and told the people that he was going.
And he told my sister where my mother and I were.
We were at the farm.
And he-- he told her that he's going to try to get there.
He doesn't know how successful he'll be, but he'll try to get there.
So my father made it.
To the farm.
And of course, Pavlo wanted to get rid of us.
And I don't blame him.
He said, take your wife and child and go, because I'm afraid.
I have children.
I have a wife, and I have three children.
And my wife is driving me crazy.
And I'm a poor farmer.
He was poor.
He couldn't afford to feed extra people.
And my father said, you know, he looked.
He couldn't argue with him.
But then he said, you know what, today is very cold.
It's snowing.
You know, maybe we'll-- you'll go when it gets a little bit better, and the weather clears up a little bit.
Because every time, he-- he was looking at me.
And he was crying.
He always said, what did this child do to deserve?
I mean, she's innocent.
It's an innocent child.
He was a very religious man.
So-- and then he made us a hole in-- outside.
It was out-- there-- there was a haystack outside.
Where was this?
Where was it?
On the farm, in-- in Szubkow-- Szubkow.
Oh, it was in-- He made a hole.
On his farm.
There was a haystack.
He had a few haystacks.
So one of them, he made us a hole, and we crawled in, the three of us.
And we were there for maybe a few months.
Then the Germans came looking for Jews, and they had long poles-- long poles.
And they would stick the poles in, you know, to the haystacks.
And one time, my father's skin was scratched.
That's how close.
Another-- another little, you know, you know-- he would have-- they would have caught us.
And-- and I remember one thing, that the minute the Germans would come looking for the-- you know, for Jews in the farm, the first thing I would do is put on my shoes.
I was like obsessed with my shoes.
I had high-- high boots, like.
And I used to start lacing them up.
I was afraid if they catch us, I won't have time to put on my shoes.
They're going to get angry at me.
They're going to hit me, because they have to wait for me.
So I was ready.
The minute the Germans came on the farm, my shoes were on.
I started tying up my shoes.
And so afterwards, he took us out from there, and he took-- put us in the barn.
He had a barn, you know, where you can put like horses and-- in the middle.
And there was hay on each side of the barn.
And he made us a hole in one of they hay-- you know, in-- in the hay.
And slowly, but surely, we stayed there for 18 months.
And every few days, we were going to leave.
He was-- told us to go.
And I can't blame him.
He said, you know, tomorrow, it's going to be better, the weather.
But he knew that the minute that he would let us go, we would be killed within maybe hours, not even days.
And there was a party that was called [NON-ENGLISH].
I'm not sure if you've ever heard this word-- Ukrainian that called themselves [NON-ENGLISH].
And they were worse than Hitler, worse than the Germans.
They said a bullet was too good for a Jew.
When they would catch Jews, they would just cut them up to pieces.
It was awful.
So we were more fearful of the [NON-ENGLISH] than of the Germans.
At least, you get a bullet, and it's all over with.
So every time we would get ready to leave, he would look at me, and he would cry.
He was a very kind-- a very religious person.
And he said, what did this child do to deserve?
And he-- and so start making-- he would-- he would make excuses, and he would say, oh, it's so cold.
It's snowing, or this, or that.
Let's do it tomorrow, and tomorrow never came, 'cause he-- What, Pavlo was making excuses or your father?
No, Pavlo.
My father couldn't say anything.
Oh, yeah, Pavlo was making excuses for himself.
He couldn't-- he just-- he knew.
And there was so many times.
And there were so many times that the Germans and the Ukrainians would come on the farm looking for Jews.
And-- and on his farm, he had a brother on the same farm that never knew that we existed, that he would have given us away in a minute.
So it was just his children and his wife knew that.
And this is how we-- we spend 18 months-- unbelievable.
